_meta.note: 61 controls from docs/reference/fair-cam-controls-library.csv, with manually-curated NIST CSF 1.1 / CIS v8 framework tags. Every entry's FAIR-CAM claims are crosswalk-validated (NIST+CIS) by tests/migrations/test_control_library_seed.py. Tags were authored validate-not-derive: a tag is present only if the control genuinely carries it in NIST/CIS independent of the FAIR-CAM function it grounds. Entries 62-63 (managed-detection-response, external-attack-surface-management) are JSON-only #459 gap additions, not in the FAIR-CAM CSV.
_meta.defaults_note: capability/coverage/reliability defaults (0.7 / 0.8 / 0.8, or capability=null for ELAPSED_TIME/CURRENCY units) are REFERENCE placeholders inherited from the importer default-by-unit convention. They are authored values, NOT crosswalk-derived (the crosswalk is binary and supplies no magnitude), and are surfaced unconfirmed for per-org tuning at adopt time.
_meta.license_note: No third-party license is claimed over the factual FAIR-CAM framework mappings used for validation; they are mapping relationships referenced from the FAIR Institute's published crosswalks. See data/seed_framework_crosswalk.NOTICE.md.

claim_drops.reason: dsc_prev_incentives is only groundable by NIST ID.SC-3 (supply-chain contracts) and lec_prev_deterrence only by PR.DS-5 (DLP) — neither tag is one an Acceptable Use Policy would carry independently (D2 / Meth-B1/B2). Contrived tags avoided.
dsc_prev_incentives is only groundable by NIST ID.SC-3 (supply-chain contracts), which Asset Management would not carry independently of the function (D2 / Meth-B1).

vmc_id_control_monitoring dropped: CRQM quantifies/reports risk and informs control-improvement decisions (PR.IP-7 grounds vmc_corr_implementation) but does not itself monitor control state; DE.CM-8/ID.SC-4 are scanning/supplier-assessment controls CRQM would not carry. No defensible tag.

dsc_prev_sa_reporting dropped: only NIST reporting/comms subcategories (RS.CO-*, DE.DP-4, RC.CO-3) ground it; an SWG is an inline web-filtering gateway, not an internal reporting control — it would not carry those tags independently.

dsc_prev_sa_reporting dropped: only reporting/comms subcategories ground it; a 'security conscious personnel' posture is an awareness/culture outcome, not a reporting capability — no defensible reporting tag.
dsc_prev_sa_reporting dropped: only RS.CO-*/DE.DP-4/RC.CO-3 ground it; an external security rating service is an analysis/monitoring input (ID.SC-4, RS.AN-2), not an internal reporting control.

lec_prev_resistance dropped (TPRM is a governance/assessment program, not a technical resistance control — PR.AC-*/PR.DS-* would be contrived); dsc_prev_sa_data_controls dropped (that function covers INTERNAL control-state inventories e.g. CIS 5.1/6.6, whereas TPRM tracks third-party-risk data — no defensible tag).

vmc_prev_reduce_variance_prob dropped: an awareness/culture control does not reduce a control's change/variance frequency (B-METH-1). lec_prev_resistance was PREVIOUSLY dropped here because the tags then considered (PR.AC-1/PR.IP-3/CIS 16.10) were contrived for an awareness posture; it is RE-ADDED in #437 rollout T2 via the GENUINE home CIS 14.2 (Train Workforce Members to Recognize Social Engineering Attacks) + a reviewed crosswalk-seed extension 14.2 -> lec_prev_resistance, so no contrived tag is resurrected.

vmc_corr_implementation dropped (#437 rollout T2, audit mis-channel): SCP reduces the exploitable-defect density of its OWN code (the software application is the ASSET) = LEC Resistance per rubric §4-I1 Example-3; it does not sustain or correct a SEPARATE degraded control, which is what VMC-Correction models (§4-I1 Example-2). SCP now scores via lec_prev_resistance; dsc_prev_defined_expectations (coding standards) is retained label-only.

vmc_id_control_monitoring + vmc_corr_implementation dropped (#437 rollout T3, non-faithful VMC-on-self boilerplate): the 'monitoring/auditing' in the description is self-operation on the encryption control itself, not identification/correction of a SEPARATE degraded control, which is what VMC models (rubric §4-I1). The pair was also partial (vmc_corr_implementation null-capability) so it scored $0. dsc_prev_defined_expectations dropped: encryption is a technical resistance control, not a decision-support capability (§2.7 do-not-author-DSC-chasing-value); it was a 1-of-9 label-only DSC that added no score. DRE still SCORES via the genuine lec_prev_resistance; lec_resp_loss_reduction (encrypted-data safe-harbor loss-magnitude reduction, null-capability pending a T4 cited figure) is retained as a GENUINE channel.

vmc_id_control_monitoring + vmc_corr_implementation dropped (#437 rollout T3, non-faithful VMC-on-self boilerplate): same rationale as DRE — encryption monitoring/auditing is self-operation, not VMC identification/correction of a separate control (rubric §4-I1). Partial pair (vmc_corr_implementation null-capability) scored $0. DTE still SCORES via the genuine lec_prev_resistance.

vmc_prev_reduce_variance_prob dropped (#437 rollout T3, double-scorer): NAC posture-gates ACCESS — that behavior is lec_prev_avoidance (already assigned + scoring, removing-contact per rubric §4-I1). NAC does not reduce the frequency/probability of changes that introduce a SEPARATE control's variance (VMC-Prevention); the variance channel double-counts the same access-gating mechanism the kept avoidance channel already captures. No distinct change-frequency-reduction home in the crosswalk. NAC still SCORES via lec_prev_avoidance.

vmc_prev_reduce_variance_prob dropped (#437 rollout T3, double-scorer, borderline rubric I5): the 'entitlement drift' UAC governs is UAC's OWN entitlements (the ASSET), not a SEPARATE control's change-variance frequency (VMC-Prevention); it double-counts the access-restriction mechanism the kept lec_prev_resistance already captures. vmc_id_control_monitoring + vmc_corr_implementation are RETAINED (genuine partial meta pair, $0, faithful) and dsc_prev_defined_expectations is retained (harmless label-only). UAC still SCORES via lec_prev_resistance.

vmc_corr_implementation dropped (#437 rollout T3, mis-channel): CRQM SELECTS/PRIORITIZES treatments (decision-support = DSC), it does not IMPLEMENT corrections — vmc_corr_implementation models the ELAPSED_TIME time-to-restore-a-degraded-control, which is a remediation-execution capability CRQM does not carry. The DSC triad (dsc_prev_sa_reporting/dsc_prev_sa_analysis/dsc_prev_defined_expectations) is retained as the genuine decision-support channel. CRQM stays NON-SCORING (genuinely-meta residual, disposition B, pending a direct channel in #439); this is a faithfulness cleanup, not a scoring change.

corrections: CSV->FAIR-CAM correction (system-capacity-planning): CSV mapped to dsc_prev_defined_expectations; corrected to dsc_prev_ensure_capability via NIST PR.DS-4 (methodology recommendation, P2b Task 5).
_meta.iso_27001_note: iso_27001_controls tags use ISO/IEC 27001:2013 Annex A numbering (A.x.x.x), NOT the 2022 renumbering. They are carried forward-compat only and are EXCLUDED from crosswalk validation (gate F-4, see services/control_library_validation.py SEEDED_FRAMEWORKS); re-mapping to 27001:2022 + un-gating is tracked in #476.

entries.name: Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)
entries.description: Acceptable Use Policy as a security control refers to establishing clear guidelines and policies regarding the appropriate and authorized use of organizational technology resources. These guidelines outline permissible actions and behaviors for employees, contractors, and other users when accessing, utilizing, and interacting with digital assets such as computers, networks, software, and data. The purpose of implementing Acceptable Use policies is multifaceted. Firstly, they mitigate security risks by defining boundaries and expectations for users, helping to prevent accidental or intentional breaches of security protocols. Organizations can reduce the likelihood of unauthorized access, data breaches, and other cybersecurity incidents by clearly outlining what constitutes acceptable behavior. Secondly, Acceptable Use policies promote the responsible and efficient use of technology resources. By providing guidelines on appropriate use, organizations can ensure that their systems and networks are used for legitimate business purposes, thereby minimizing the risk of productivity loss or resource misuse. Moreover, acceptable use policies play a crucial role in promoting an organization's culture of security awareness. By educating users about their responsibilities and the potential consequences of non-compliance, organizations can foster a mindset of vigilance and accountability among employees, contractors, and other stakeholders. Consequences for not adhering to acceptable use policy are essential for reinforcing compliance and maintaining accountability. Organizations usually imply disciplinary actions, such as warnings, retraining, suspension of privileges, or even termination of employment, depending on the severity of the violation as a breach of acceptable usage conduct. By enforcing consequences consistently and fairly, organizations demonstrate the seriousness of security policies and deter individuals from engaging in risky or unauthorized behaviors. Effective implementation of Acceptable use policy as a security control typically involves several key components, including: - Policy Development: Developing comprehensive policies that clearly outline acceptable behaviors, responsibilities, and consequences for non-compliance. - Policy Acknowledgement: All users must acknowledge their understanding of the acceptable use policy and agree to comply with its provisions, typically through a formal acknowledgment process. - User Education and Awareness: Providing training and awareness programs to educate users about the Acceptable Usage policies and their importance in maintaining a secure computing environment. - Monitoring and Enforcement: Implementing mechanisms to monitor user activities and enforce compliance with Acceptable Usage policies, such as logging and auditing systems, access controls, and disciplinary measures for violations. - Regular Review and Updates: Review and update Acceptable Usage policies to adapt to changing technologies, threats, and organizational needs. In Summary, the Acceptable Use Policy is a set of guidelines and rules organizations establish to define the acceptable use of their information technology resources by employees, contractors, and other authorized users. This policy outlines the permitted activities and behaviors regarding the organization's IT assets, networks, and data. It covers internet and email usage, software installation, data privacy, confidentiality, and security practices. Acceptable Usage control aims to ensure that employees understand their responsibilities and obligations when using organizational resources, mitigate risks associated with misuse or abuse of technology, and protect the organization's reputation, assets, and data.

entries.name: Application Performance Monitoring (APM)
entries.description: Application Performance Monitoring (APM) actively monitors the performance of software applications. It can enable the detection of anomalies or malicious activities that may indicate security threats. APM tools continuously track performance metrics, such as response times, error rates, and resource utilization, to establish a baseline of normal application behavior. Any deviations from this baseline, such as sudden spikes in traffic, unusual patterns of user behavior, or unexpected errors, can signal potential security incidents. By analyzing these performance metrics in real time, APM solutions can identify signs of suspicious activity, such as denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, data exfiltration attempts, or unauthorized access. Additionally, APM tools provide insights into the performance of third-party components and application dependencies. This enables organizations to monitor and mitigate security risks associated with these components. Security teams can use APM logs and performance data to reconstruct the events leading up to the incident, determine the root cause, and assess the impact on application performance. This information facilitates practical incident response efforts and helps organizations improve their security posture against similar threats in the future. In summary, APM serves as a valuable security control by detecting anomalies early, identifying malicious activity, monitoring third-party components, supporting incident response efforts, and integrating with broader security monitoring and response systems. By leveraging APM as part of their cybersecurity strategy, organizations can enhance their ability to detect, respond to, and mitigate security threats targeting their applications and IT infrastructure.

entries.name: Asset Management (AM)
entries.description: Asset Management is one of the strategic and tactical cybersecurity controls, encompassing the organized oversight and administration of an organization's assets. This control involves systematically identifying, cataloging, and managing all digital resources, including hardware, software, data, and network components. At its core, Asset Management ensures that organizations maintain a comprehensive inventory of their assets. This inventory is the foundation for understanding the organization's digital footprint and forms the basis for effective risk management. Asset Management also involves classifying assets based on their importance and sensitivity to the organization. Throughout their lifecycle, assets undergo various stages, from acquisition to disposal. Security teams can better assess potential vulnerabilities and threats by knowing what assets exist and where they are located. Asset Management control also enables an incident response and forensic investigation. By correlating asset information with security logs and event data, organizations can identify affected assets, assess the scope of the incident, and determine the root cause. It enables effective containment, remediation, and recovery efforts to minimize the impact of security incidents. Organizations gain deeper insights into security threats and incidents across their environment by correlating asset information with other security telemetry, such as Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) data. This integration enables faster detection and response to cyber threats, strengthening the organization's security posture. In summary, Asset Management is a security control that provides visibility and governance over an organization's assets. By effectively managing assets throughout their lifecycle, organizations can mitigate security risks, maintain compliance, enforce access controls, and respond effectively to security incidents, safeguarding their valuable digital assets and ensuring their infrastructure's confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

entries.name: Background Verification (BGV)
entries.description: Background Verification control aims to validate the integrity and trustworthiness of individuals with access to sensitive information or critical systems within an organization. This control involves thorough checks on the background, qualifications, and history of employees, contractors, vendors, and other personnel to ensure they meet the organization's security standards and requirements. This verification helps organizations assess the reliability and credibility of individuals and identify any potential risks or concerns that may threaten the organization's security. It is vital in mitigating insider threats and preventing unauthorized access to sensitive information or systems. By conducting background checks on individuals before granting them access privileges, organizations can reduce the likelihood of malicious activities from insiders with malicious intent, such as data breaches, sabotage, or espionage. Moreover, Background Verification helps ensure compliance with regulatory requirements and industry standards, which often mandate thorough screening of individuals with access to sensitive data or critical infrastructure. By adhering to these requirements, organizations demonstrate their commitment to safeguarding sensitive information and maintaining the trust of customers, partners, and stakeholders. In addition to pre-employment background checks, organizations may also conduct periodic re-verification or screening of personnel to ensure ongoing compliance with security policies and standards. This continuous monitoring helps organizations identify changes in personnel's circumstances or behaviors that may pose security risks and take appropriate actions to mitigate them. In summary, Background Verification is a proactive security measure to mitigate insider threats, prevent unauthorized access, and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements. By thoroughly vetting individuals with access to sensitive information or critical systems, organizations can strengthen their security posture and protect their assets from potential security breaches and insider attacks.

entries.name: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR)
entries.description: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR) control enables organizations to ensure uninterrupted business functions during an adverse event such as a cyber attack, system failure, or natural disaster. This control involves developing comprehensive strategies and procedures to mitigate the impact of such events and minimize downtime, thereby safeguarding the organization's assets, reputation, and ability to deliver products and services. The Business Continuity Planning aspect of BCDR involves identifying critical business processes, dependencies, and resources and developing contingency plans and alternate solutions to ensure continued operation. By implementing redundant systems, backup facilities, and alternative workflows, organizations can minimize the impact of disruptions and maintain essential services to customers, employees, and stakeholders. The Disaster Recovery aspect of BCDR planning involves restoring IT systems, infrastructure, and data following a disruptive event. It includes developing data backup, replication, and recovery procedures and testing and validating these processes to ensure their effectiveness. Organizations can quickly recover critical systems and data and resume normal operations with minimal downtime by implementing robust backup and recovery mechanisms. In summary, Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery planning is a security control encompassing strategies, procedures, and measures to ensure the resilience and continuity of business operations in the face of disruptive events. By developing a proactive response and recovery process, organizations can minimize the magnitude of disruptions and protect their business-critical assets, and thus extend comfort and confidence to the customers, partners, and stakeholders, even during the most challenging circumstances.

entries.name: Change Management (CM)
entries.description: Change Management is a security control that systematically manages and oversees changes to IT systems, applications, configurations, and processes within an organization. This control ensures that implemented changes are evaluated to minimize disruptions, mitigate risks, and reduce the potential unwanted adverse impact on the assets or IT environment. At its core, Change Management involves establishing formal processes and procedures for requesting, reviewing, approving, implementing, and documenting IT assets and infrastructure changes. This includes defining roles, responsibilities, and workflows to effectively govern the change management process. One of the main objectives of Change Management is to assess the potential impact of proposed changes on security, stability, and performance before implementing them. This process also involves conducting thorough risk assessments and impact analyses to identify potential risks, dependencies, and conflicts associated with proposed changes. By evaluating these factors, organizations can make informed decisions about whether to proceed with changes and implement appropriate mitigating controls to address identified risks. In summary, Change Management is pivotal in maintaining an organization's IT environment's confidentiality, integrity, and availability by ensuring that changes are controlled and systematic. By establishing formal processes, conducting risk assessments, implementing appropriate controls, and fostering communication and coordination among stakeholders, organizations can minimize the risk of disruptions, prevent unauthorized changes, and maintain the reliability and availability of IT systems and services.

entries.name: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)
entries.description: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) control is designed to ensure cloud environments' security, compliance, and resilience. This control encompasses a set of tools, processes, and practices that enable organizations to proactively identify, assess, and remediate security risks and misconfigurations in their cloud infrastructure and services. At its core, CSPM involves continuously monitoring and evaluating the security posture of cloud environments to identify potential vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and compliance violations. This includes assessing the configuration settings, permissions, and access controls of cloud resources, such as virtual machines, storage buckets, databases, and networking components, against security best practices, industry standards, and regulatory requirements. CSPM tools enable organizations to identify and prioritize security risks based on their severity and potential impact by often leveraging automation and machine learning capabilities to analyze vast amounts of data and provide real-time insights into security issues. One of the main objectives of CSPM is to ensure that cloud environments adhere to security and compliance policies, standards, and regulations. It compares cloud configurations against predefined security baselines and regulatory requirements, such as CIS benchmarks, NIST guidelines, GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI DSS. Organizations can take corrective actions to bring cloud environments into compliance and mitigate potential security risks by identifying deviations from these standards. CSPM also involves implementing proactive security controls to prevent and mitigate security threats and attacks in cloud environments. It includes enforcing least privilege access controls, implementing encryption and data protection measures, configuring network security groups and firewall rules, and monitoring for abnormal behavior and suspicious activities. Furthermore, CSPM includes mechanisms for continuous monitoring, auditing, and reporting of security posture and compliance status in cloud environments. It allows organizations to track changes in their cloud infrastructure, detect security incidents, and generate compliance reports for audits and regulatory inspections. In summary, Cloud Security Posture Management control helps organizations ensure their cloud environments' security, compliance, and resilience. By continuously monitoring, assessing, and remediating security risks and misconfigurations, CSPM enables organizations to maintain a strong security posture in the cloud, protect sensitive data, and mitigate the risk of security breaches and compliance violations.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate, MITRE grounds channel not magnitude): Mechanism: CSPM detects and re-privatizes publicly-exposed cloud assets — public object-storage buckets, open security-group ingress (0.0.0.0/0 on RDP/SSH), over-broad egress — removing unauthenticated contact between external threat agents and cloud data stores. Asset = cloud data stores/compute; susceptibility = network reachability/public exposure; threat = external actors enumerating exposed cloud resources. Channel grounding: MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise T1530 (Data from Cloud Storage) + mitigation M1037 (Filter Network Traffic), https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1530/ (accessed 2026-06-30); CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark — S3 Block Public Access. Magnitude 0.6 is a conservative expert estimate — MITRE provides qualitative mitigation coverage, not a numeric population reduction percentage; no primary source maps avoidance coverage to a measured [0,1] value.

Mechanism (expert-estimate, MITRE grounds channel not magnitude): Mechanism: CSPM enforces encryption-at-rest, least-privilege IAM, and configuration hardening on cloud resources, reducing the probability that a threat agent who reaches the asset can exploit stored data or escalate. Asset = cloud data/compute; susceptibility = exploitability after contact (unencrypted data, over-permissioned roles); threat = credential abuse / data theft. Channel grounding: MITRE ATT&CK T1530 + mitigations M1041 (Encrypt Sensitive Information) and M1022 (Restrict File and Directory Permissions), https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1530/ (accessed 2026-06-30). Magnitude 0.5 is a conservative expert estimate — resistance is secondary to avoidance for CSPM; MITRE provides qualitative mitigation notes, not a measured reduction percentage.

Mechanism (meta, non-scoring): CSPM continuously monitors configuration drift of OTHER security controls (logging enablement, detector state, baseline policies). Per rubric §4 Example 2 this is VMC Identification, not LEC. Value 0.7 is an expert estimate — no population control-health study exists.

entries.name: Compliance Audit (COMP)
entries.description: Compliance Audit is a security control that systematically reviews and evaluates an organization's adherence to regulatory requirements, industry standards, and internal policies related to information security and privacy. This control ensures that the organization operates per applicable laws, regulations, and standards and maintains safeguards to protect sensitive information and assets. At its core, Compliance Audit involves conducting periodic assessments and examinations to verify that the organization's security practices, procedures, and controls align with established compliance frameworks and requirements. This includes reviewing documentation, conducting interviews, and performing technical assessments to assess the effectiveness and maturity of the organization's security program. Compliance Audit encompasses various aspects of information security, including data protection, access controls, risk management, incident response, and privacy. Organizations must demonstrate that they have implemented appropriate safeguards to protect sensitive data, prevent unauthorized access, and respond effectively to security incidents. One of the main objectives of Compliance Audit is to identify gaps, deficiencies, and areas of non-compliance within the organization's security posture. Organizations can take corrective actions to address deficiencies and mitigate potential risks by identifying non-compliance areas. Compliance Audit also involves documenting audit findings, recommendations, and remediation plans to ensure accountability and transparency. This includes preparing audit reports, recording evidence of compliance, and tracking remediation efforts to address identified deficiencies. Effective communication and collaboration with stakeholders are essential to ensure relevant parties understand, accept, and act upon audit findings. In summary, Compliance Audit is a security control that helps organizations verify and validate their adherence to regulatory requirements, industry standards, and internal information security and privacy policies. Organizations can identify and address compliance issues, mitigate risks, and strengthen their overall security posture by conducting regular assessments and examinations, documenting audit findings, and collaborating with stakeholders.

entries.name: Cyber Insurance (CI)
entries.description: Cyber Insurance is a financial security control that organizations can leverage to mitigate the potential financial losses associated with cybersecurity incidents and data breaches. It involves purchasing insurance policies specifically designed to cover expenses related to cyber incidents, including data breaches, ransomware attacks, business interruptions, and legal liabilities. At its core, Cyber Insurance provides organizations with financial protection and risk transfer mechanisms in the event of a cybersecurity incident. It typically covers a range of expenses, such as the costs of forensic investigations, data restoration, legal fees, regulatory fines, notification expenses, and extortion payments. Cyber Insurance helps organizations recover more quickly and resume normal operations with minimal disruption by mitigating the financial impact of cyber incidents. While preventive measures and security controls can reduce the likelihood of cyber incidents, they cannot eliminate the risk. Cyber Insurance provides a safety net for organizations, offering financial assistance when preventive measures fail and incidents occur. Cyber Insurance policies are tailored to organizations' unique needs and risk profiles, considering factors such as industry sector, size, revenue, and operations. Organizations can customize their coverage based on specific risk exposures and budgetary constraints, ensuring adequate protection against potential cyber threats. In summary, Cyber Insurance is a security control that helps organizations mitigate the financial impact of cybersecurity incidents and data breaches. By providing financial protection, promoting cybersecurity best practices, and offering access to resources and services, Cyber Insurance enables organizations to manage cyber risks more effectively and recover quickly from cyber incidents.

entries.name: Cyber Risk Quantification and Management (CRQM)
entries.description: Cyber Risk Quantification and Management control is focused on assessing, measuring, and managing the cyber risk within an organization. This control involves systematic processes and methodologies to quantify the potential impact of cyber risks on the organization's assets, operations, and reputation, as well as strategies to mitigate these risks effectively. At its core, Cyber Risk Quantification and Management begins with identifying and assessing the various cyber risks that the organization faces. It includes identifying potential threats, identifying a method the threat actor would use, and determining the impact the threat actor would have on business resources such as customer or PII data. One of the main components of Cyber Risk Quantification and Management is quantifying the financial impact of cyber risks on the organization. It involves estimating the potential costs associated with data breaches, system outages, regulatory fines, legal liabilities, reputational damage, and other adverse consequences of cyber incidents. By quantifying these potential costs, organizations can make informed decisions about investing in cybersecurity measures and risk mitigation strategies. Cyber Risk Quantification and Management also involves implementing measures to reduce the likelihood of the loss event occurring and reducing the magnitude of loss if the loss event occurs. Furthermore, Cyber Risk Quantification and Management encompasses ongoing monitoring and review of cyber risks to ensure that mitigation measures remain adequate and relevant over time. This involves monitoring emerging threats, changes in the organization's risk profile, and the effectiveness of existing controls to adapt and refine risk management strategies accordingly. Collaboration and communication among stakeholders are essential components of Cyber Risk Quantification and Management. It includes engaging with executive leadership, IT and security teams, risk management professionals, legal counsel, and insurance providers to ensure alignment of risk management strategies with business objectives and regulatory requirements. Organizations can effectively identify, assess, and mitigate cyber risks by fostering collaboration and communication. In summary, Cyber Risk Quantification and Management is a control that enables organizations to identify, assess, and manage cyber risks effectively. By quantifying the financial impact of cyber risks, implementing risk mitigation measures, and fostering collaboration among stakeholders, organizations can strengthen their resilience to cyber threats, protect critical assets and operations, and maintain trust with their customers, partners, and stakeholders.

entries.name: Data at Rest Encryption (DRE)
entries.description: Data at Rest Encryption control enables encryption of sensitive data stored on physical or digital storage devices to protect it from unauthorized access or disclosure. This control ensures that the data remains unintelligible without the appropriate decryption key, even if an attacker gains access to the storage device. At its core, Data at Rest Encryption aims to safeguard sensitive information, such as personally identifiable information (PII), financial records, intellectual property, and other confidential data. At the same time, it resides in storage, whether it's on a hard drive, a database, or in the cloud. This encryption process scrambles the data using complex algorithms, rendering it unreadable to anyone who does not have the decryption key. Data at Rest Encryption also helps organizations maintain compliance with regulatory requirements and industry standards that mandate the protection of sensitive data. For example, regulations such as GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI DSS require organizations to implement appropriate safeguards to protect sensitive data, including encryption of data at rest. Furthermore, Data at Rest Encryption enhances the security of data backups and archives by ensuring that sensitive information remains protected even when it's not actively being used or accessed. It is significant for organizations that store sensitive data for extended periods, as it reduces the risk of data breaches and unauthorized access over time. Implementing Data at Rest Encryption requires organizations to establish encryption policies, select appropriate encryption algorithms and key management practices, and deploy encryption solutions across their storage infrastructure. It's essential to carefully manage encryption keys to ensure secure and reliable access to encrypted data while preventing unauthorized access. Regular monitoring and auditing of Data at Rest Encryption processes and practices are essential to ensure compliance, detect potential vulnerabilities or misconfigurations, and address security incidents promptly. In summary, Data at Rest Encryption control helps organizations protect sensitive data stored on storage devices from unauthorized access or disclosure. By encrypting data at rest, organizations can reduce the impact and potentially prevent a successful data exfiltration attack, maintain compliance with regulatory requirements, and safeguard sensitive information from prying eyes and malicious actors.

entries.name: Data Backup and Recovery (DBR)
entries.description: Data Backup and Recovery is a control that enables organizations to create copies of critical data and establish procedures to restore this data in the event of data loss, data corruption, or cyberattacks. This control ensures data assets' availability, integrity, and resilience, enabling organizations to recover quickly from disruptions and maintain business continuity. At its core, Data Backup and Recovery involves regularly backing up essential data, such as customer records, financial information, intellectual property, and operational data, to secondary storage locations, such as backup servers, tape drives, or cloud storage services. These backups serve as redundant copies of data that organizations can use to restore information in case the primary data sources become inaccessible or compromised. One of the main objectives of Data Backup and Recovery is to mitigate the risk of losing data events such as hardware failures, software errors, natural disasters, human errors, and malicious activities such as ransomware attacks. Implementing Data Backup and Recovery requires organizations to establish backup policies, procedures, and schedules tailored to their business needs and risk profile. It includes identifying critical data sources, defining backup frequency and retention periods, selecting appropriate backup technologies and storage solutions, and regularly testing backup and recovery processes to ensure their effectiveness. Regular monitoring, testing, and auditing of Data Backup and Recovery processes are essential to verify the integrity of backup data, validate recovery procedures, and identify and address any gaps or deficiencies in the backup infrastructure. In summary, Data Backup and Recovery is a security control that helps organizations protect critical data assets, mitigate the risk of data loss or data corruption, and maintain business continuity in the face of disruptions. By establishing robust backup strategies, implementing reliable recovery procedures, and adhering to best practices in data protection, organizations can ensure the availability, integrity, and resilience of their data assets and safeguard their operations against potential threats and vulnerabilities.

entries.name: Data Classification and Handling (DCH)
entries.description: Data classification and handling controls aim to protect sensitive information from unauthorized access, disclosure, or misuse. It involves categorizing data based on sensitivity, value, regulatory requirements, etc. This process helps identify the appropriate level of protection (CIA requirements) needed for different data types. By assigning classification labels, organizations can apply security measures commensurate with the level of risk posed by each data category. Handling procedures govern how data is collected, stored, processed, transmitted, and disposed of throughout its lifecycle. These procedures outline security protocols and controls to ensure that data remains secure and confidential throughout the lifecycle. Furthermore, data handling practices include guidelines for sharing data with third parties, such as vendors or partners, to minimize the risk of data breaches or compliance violations. Additionally, procedures for data retention and disposal help organizations manage data in accordance with legal and regulatory requirements while mitigating the risk of data exposure. In Summary, data classification and handling enable organizations to protect sensitive information, maintain compliance with applicable regulations, and mitigate the risk of data breaches or unauthorized access. Organizations can ensure their data assets' confidentiality, integrity, and availability by implementing robust classification and handling procedures. Note: Data classification and Data handling are two controls and different processes; we have combined them as one control to reduce the redundancy between both procedures and incorporate implementation-level practicality. For example, an organization may define the data classification requirements before deciding on the data handling requirements within its lifecycle. Regulatory requirements for data handling may also inspire the data classification requirements. However, organizations may also consider these as separate controls as per their governance objectives.

entries.name: Data in Transit Encryption (DTE)
entries.description: Data in Transit Encryption is a security control that encrypts sensitive data while we transmit it between devices, networks, or systems. This control ensures that data remains confidential and unintelligible to prevent unauthorized usage during transmission over potentially insecure communication channels, such as the Internet or wireless networks. At its core, Data in Transit Encryption utilizes encryption algorithms to scramble data into an unreadable format before transmitting it from one endpoint to another. This encryption process ensures that even if intercepted by unauthorized parties, the data remains unintelligible and can only be accessed using an appropriate decryption key. One of the main objectives of Data in Transit Encryption is to safeguard sensitive information, such as personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, intellectual property, and other confidential data, while transmitted across networks. This protection extends to various communication channels, including email, web browsing, file transfers, messaging applications, and remote access connections. Implementing Data in Transit Encryption involves deploying encryption technologies, such as SSL/TLS protocols, VPN (Virtual Private Network) connections, and secure communication protocols across network infrastructure and endpoints. Organizations must securely configure these encryption mechanisms and ensure that encryption keys are managed securely to prevent unauthorized access to encrypted data. Regular monitoring and auditing of Data in Transit Encryption processes and practices are essential to ensure compliance, detect potential vulnerabilities or misconfigurations, and address security incidents promptly. In summary, Data in Transit Encryption control helps organizations protect sensitive data while transmitted across networks or communication channels. By encrypting data during transit, organizations can maintain the confidentiality, integrity, and privacy of their data assets, mitigate the risk of unauthorized access or interception, and comply with regulatory requirements and industry standards for data protection.

entries.name: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
entries.description: Data Loss Prevention (DLP) enables organizations to prevent the unauthorized disclosure, leakage, or loss of sensitive information from within an organization. This control encompasses a set of policies, technologies, and processes designed to identify, monitor, and protect sensitive data wherever it resides, whether it's on-premises, in the cloud, or being transmitted across networks. At its core, Data Loss Prevention involves identifying and classifying sensitive data, such as personally identifiable information (PII), intellectual property, financial records, and confidential business data. Organizations establish policies and rules to define what constitutes sensitive data and then employ technologies such as content inspection, contextual analysis, and machine learning algorithms to detect and monitor the movement of this data within the organization's network and endpoints. One of the main objectives of Data Loss Prevention is to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data and mitigate the risk of data breaches, data leaks, or insider threats. By implementing DLP solutions, organizations can enforce policies that control the transfer, sharing, or storage of sensitive data, thereby reducing the likelihood of data loss incidents and protecting the organization's reputation and brand. Implementing Data Loss Prevention involves several key steps, including: 1. Data Discovery and Classification: Identifying and categorizing sensitive data based on its type, sensitivity, and importance to the organization. 2. Policy Creation and Enforcement: Establishing policies and rules to govern the handling, transmitting, and storing sensitive data and enforcing these policies across the organization's network and endpoints. 3. Monitoring and Detection: Deploying DLP solutions to monitor data flows, detect policy violations, and alert security teams to potential data loss incidents in real-time. 4. Incident Response and Remediation: Responding to data loss incidents promptly, investigating the root cause of breaches, and implementing remediation measures to prevent future occurrences. In summary, Data Loss Prevention control helps organizations protect sensitive data from unauthorized access, disclosure, or loss. By implementing DLP solutions, organizations can identify, monitor, and protect sensitive data wherever it resides, mitigate the risk of data breaches, comply with regulatory requirements, and safeguard their reputation and brand.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate, MITRE grounds channel not magnitude): Mechanism: DLP inspects content/context of data in motion and at rest and blocks egress of sensitive data, reducing the probability that an exfiltration attempt succeeds against the data asset. Asset = sensitive data stores/endpoints; susceptibility = exfiltratability over web/email/removable channels; threat = insider/exfiltration actors (T1048/T1567/T1041). Channel grounding: MITRE ATT&CK mitigation M1057 (Data Loss Prevention) mitigating T1048 (Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol), https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1057/ (accessed 2026-06-30); CIS Safeguard 3.13 (Deploy a Data Loss Prevention Solution). Magnitude 0.7 is a conservative expert estimate — DLP is bypassable (encryption, side channels); MITRE provides qualitative mitigation descriptions, not a measured population-level blocking percentage.

entries.name: Data Privacy Policy (PRI)
entries.description: A Data Privacy Policy is a security control that enables organizations to protect personal and sensitive information. It involves developing, implementing, and maintaining policies, procedures, and practices to ensure compliance with data privacy regulations and standards. At its core, a Data Privacy Policy focuses on understanding data flows, storage locations, access controls, and potential privacy vulnerabilities. The policy encompasses measures to establish and communicate privacy guidelines to employees, contractors, and third-party partners, emphasizing their responsibilities in handling sensitive data. It defines the requirements for implementing technical controls, such as encryption, access controls, data anonymization, and monitoring mechanisms, to safeguard data against unauthorized access, disclosure, or misuse. By establishing a robust Data Privacy Policy, organizations demonstrate their commitment to protecting individuals' privacy rights, enhancing customer trust, mitigating legal and financial risks associated with data breaches, and maintaining a positive reputation in the marketplace.

entries.name: DDoS Protection (DDOS)
entries.description: DDoS Protection, or Distributed Denial of Service Protection, is a security control designed to defend against and mitigate the impact of Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks. These attacks aim to overwhelm a targeted network, system, or service with malicious traffic, rendering it inaccessible to legitimate users and disrupting normal operations. At its core, DDoS Protection involves implementing strategies, technologies, and processes to detect, filter, and mitigate attacks in real-time, ensuring the availability and reliability of critical network resources and services. This control encompasses a range of proactive and reactive measures to prevent DDoS attacks from causing disruptions and downtime. One of the main objectives of DDoS Protection is to maintain the availability and performance of network infrastructure, web applications, and online services by defending against the debilitating effects of DDoS attacks. DDoS Protection solutions typically employ traffic analysis, rate limiting, IP filtering, and traffic diversion techniques to identify and mitigate DDoS attacks effectively. These solutions may be deployed at various points within the network infrastructure, including on-premises appliances and cloud-based platforms, or through the services of third-party DDoS mitigation providers. In summary, DDoS Protection is a security control that helps organizations defend against and mitigate the impact of DDoS attacks, ensuring the availability and reliability of network resources and online services. By implementing robust DDoS protection mechanisms and response strategies, organizations can minimize downtime, protect their reputation, and maintain the trust and confidence of customers, partners, and stakeholders.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate; NIST/MITRE grounds channel, not magnitude): on detection of a volumetric / protocol attack, DDoS Protection performs real-time traffic scrubbing / rate-limiting / anycast diversion, maintaining service availability during the attack — a containment / resilience action that limits loss magnitude (downtime) rather than terminating the event. Scores via the fully-staffed LEC detection+response pair (existing lec_det_visibility + lec_det_recognition + this tranche's lec_det_monitoring, plus this response member). Effect = availability preserved / loss magnitude reduced; threat = DDoS. Channel grounding: NIST CSF RS.MI-2 (Incidents are mitigated); MITRE ATT&CK T1498 (Network Denial of Service) + M1037 (Filter Network Traffic). Crosswalk grounding: NIST CSF RS.MI-2 (a genuine DDoS real-time-mitigation home that already crosswalks to lec_resp_resilience; no extension needed). CHANNEL CHOICE (resilience vs event_termination): JUSTIFIED ON FIDELITY — DDoS scrubbing CONTAINS/absorbs the flood to preserve availability (resilience); it does not model a time-to-terminate (ELAPSED_TIME) event, so lec_resp_resilience is the fidelity match and its magnitude is a bounded PROBABILITY expert-estimate (≤0.8), NOT a workaround for §5.3.1's ELAPSED_TIME citation requirement. NULL-SENTINEL DISCLOSURE: the co-present lec_det_monitoring capability=null is a DELIBERATE neutral default (the ELAPSED_TIME τ·ln(2) sentinel yields opeff≈0.5, non-inflating) — left as the sentinel pending a primary detection-time (MTTD) citation rather than inventing one. Magnitude 0.6 conservative expert estimate.

entries.name: Deception Technology (DT)
entries.description: Deception technology serves as an security control within organizations, aimed at detecting and mitigating cyber threats by luring attackers into deceptive traps. It involves deploying decoy systems, networks, applications, and data assets across the IT environment, mimicking genuine resources. These decoys appear valuable targets to potential attackers, enticing them to engage and reveal their presence. The primary objective of deception technology is to detect malicious activity early in the attack lifecycle before real damage occurs. Organizations can detect unauthorized access attempts, attacker lateral movement, and reconnaissance activities by strategically placing decoys throughout the network. This proactive approach enables security teams to gather valuable intelligence about attacker tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), enhancing threat detection and response capabilities. Deception technology operates on the principle of minimizing the "dwell time" of attackers within the network, reducing the likelihood of successful breaches and data exfiltration. It complements traditional security controls such as firewalls, antivirus software, and intrusion detection systems (IDS), providing an additional layer of defense against sophisticated adversaries. Furthermore, deception technology offers benefits beyond threat detection, including threat intelligence enrichment, incident response orchestration, and security posture improvement. By analyzing the interactions with decoys, organizations can gain insights into emerging threats, enhance incident response workflows, and fine-tune security strategies to protect their assets better. In summary, deception technology empowers organizations to turn the tables on attackers, transforming their network into a hostile environment where every move is met with suspicion. By leveraging deception as a proactive security measure, organizations can strengthen their cybersecurity posture and mitigate the risk of successful cyber attacks.

entries.name: Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)
entries.description: Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) is a security control focused on identifying and addressing vulnerabilities in  applications by simulating real-world attack scenarios. This control involves automated scanning and testing of web, mobile, or thick-client applications to uncover security flaws, weaknesses, and misconfigurations that attackers could exploit. At its core, Dynamic Application Security Testing aims to proactively identify and remediate security vulnerabilities within applications before malicious actors can exploit them. Unlike static code analysis, which examines the source code of an application for vulnerabilities, DAST evaluates the application in its running state by sending specially crafted requests and payloads to identify weaknesses in its defenses. One of the main objectives of DAST is to identify common security vulnerabilities and weaknesses in applications, such as SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), authentication flaws, and insecure direct object references. By systematically scanning and testing applications for these vulnerabilities, organizations can identify and remediate security issues before they are exploited by attackers, reducing the risk of data breaches and unauthorized access. DAST solutions typically interact with web applications through their user interfaces, emulating the behavior of an attacker attempting to exploit vulnerabilities. These solutions perform dynamic analysis of the application's runtime behavior, identifying potential security weaknesses and generating reports detailing the findings, including recommendations for remediation. In summary, Dynamic Application Security Testing is a security control that helps organizations identify and remediate vulnerabilities within applications to mitigate the risk of data breaches and unauthorized access. By conducting regular DAST scans, organizations can proactively identify and address security weaknesses, comply with regulatory requirements, and enhance the overall security posture of their web applications.

entries.name: Email Security and Protection (ESP)
entries.description: Email Security and Protection is a security control to safeguard organizations against email-borne threats, such as phishing attacks, malware distribution, spam, and data leakage. This control encompasses a range of technologies, policies, and practices designed to detect, prevent, and mitigate the risks associated with email-based threats. At its core, email security and protection involves implementing measures to secure the email infrastructure, filter incoming and outgoing email traffic, authenticate senders, and educate users about best practices for email security. The goal is to ensure email communication confidentiality, integrity, and availability within the organization and protect against external threats targeting email systems. One of the main objectives of Email Security and Protection is to detect and block malicious emails before they reach end-users inboxes. It includes deploying email filtering solutions that scan incoming emails for indicators of phishing, malware, malicious attachments, suspicious links, and other forms of malicious content. Email Security and Protection also involves implementing authentication mechanisms, such as SPF (Sender Policy Framework), DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail), and DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance), and verifying the authenticity of email senders and preventing email spoofing and domain impersonation attacks. These authentication protocols help organizations ascertain the legitimacy of email messages and detect forged or fraudulent emails. In summary, Email Security and Protection is a security control that helps organizations defend against email-borne threats and protect the integrity and confidentiality of email communications. By implementing robust email security measures, organizations can reduce the risk of phishing attacks, malware infections, and data breaches stemming from email-related vulnerabilities, safeguarding their sensitive information and maintaining the trust and confidence of their stakeholders.

entries.name: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
entries.description: Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) is a security control that protects endpoints from advanced cyber threats and malicious activities. EDR solutions continuously monitor endpoint devices for signs of suspicious behavior, unauthorized access, and potential security breaches, enabling rapid detection, investigation, and response to security incidents. At its core, Endpoint Detection and Response combines advanced endpoint telemetry, behavior analysis, and threat intelligence to identify and mitigate security threats at the endpoint level. Unlike traditional antivirus solutions that rely on signature-based detection, EDR solutions leverage advanced methods, such as machine learning, anomaly detection, and behavioral analysis, to identify and respond to unknown or zero-day threats. One of the main objectives of Endpoint Detection and Response is to provide real-time visibility into endpoint activity and security events, allowing security teams to detect and investigate potential threats quickly. By continuously monitoring endpoint devices for indicators of compromise, unusual behavior, or malicious activity, EDR solutions enable organizations to detect and respond to security incidents promptly, minimizing the impact of cyber attacks. In summary, Endpoint Detection and Response is a security control that helps organizations protect endpoint devices from advanced cyber threats and respond effectively to security incidents. By continuously monitoring endpoint activity, detecting indicators of compromise, and enabling rapid incident response, EDR solutions enhance the security posture of organizations and reduce the risk of data breaches and cyber attacks targeting endpoint devices.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate; NIST/MITRE grounds channel, not magnitude): on detection, EDR isolates / quarantines the endpoint and terminates malicious processes, containing the incident and limiting loss magnitude. Scores ONLY via the fully-staffed LEC detection+response pair (all 3 detection members already present + this response member). Effect = reduced loss magnitude via containment; threat = malware / intrusion. Channel grounding: NIST CSF RS.MI-2 (Incidents are mitigated) — the 'Response' function un-authored in the prior detection-only mapping; MITRE ATT&CK response (host / network isolation). Crosswalk grounding: NIST CSF RS.MI-2. CHANNEL CHOICE (resilience vs event_termination) — JUSTIFIED ON FIDELITY, not to dodge a citation: EDR's response is endpoint ISOLATION / quarantine (a containment / resilience-flavored action that limits blast radius and loss magnitude), which is the fidelity match for lec_resp_resilience; lec_resp_event_termination models the time-to-terminate an in-progress event (ELAPSED_TIME) and is a poorer fidelity match for host-isolation containment. The resilience magnitude is a bounded PROBABILITY expert-estimate (<=0.8) per the standard channel semantics, not a workaround for §5.3.1's ELAPSED_TIME citation requirement. NULL-SENTINEL DISCLOSURE: the co-present lec_det_monitoring capability=null is a DELIBERATE neutral default — the ELAPSED_TIME tau*ln(2) sentinel yields opeff≈0.5 (neutral / non-inflating), so activating this det+resp pair does NOT let an un-cited monitoring-time inflate the score. It is left as the sentinel (not invented) pending a primary detection-time (MTTD) citation, disclosed here rather than silent. Magnitude 0.6 conservative expert estimate.

entries.name: File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)
entries.description: File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) is a security control designed to monitor and track changes to critical files and system configurations in real time. This control helps organizations detect unauthorized modifications, tampering, or corruption of files and configurations, which could indicate potential security breaches, malware infections, or insider threats. At its core, File Integrity Monitoring involves continuously monitoring the integrity of files, directories, and system configurations on servers, workstations, and other critical endpoints. By comparing the current state of files and configurations to a known baseline or predefined security policy, FIM solutions can identify unauthorized changes or deviations from the expected state, enabling security teams to respond promptly to potential security incidents. One of the key objectives of File Integrity Monitoring is to detect and alert unauthorized changes to critical files and configurations, such as system files, application binaries, configuration files, and registry settings. By monitoring file attributes such as file size, permissions, checksums, and timestamps, FIM solutions can detect alterations made by malicious actors attempting to modify or manipulate system resources. In summary, File Integrity Monitoring is a security control that helps organizations detect and respond to unauthorized changes to critical files and system configurations. By continuously monitoring file integrity, FIM solutions provide organizations with the visibility and control necessary to protect against insider threats, malware infections, and unauthorized access to sensitive data, enhancing the organization's overall security posture.

entries.name: Hardened Cloud (HAC)
entries.description: Hardened Cloud refers to the application of secure configuration settings to cloud technologies, such as infrastructure as a service (IaaS), platform as a service (PaaS), and software as a service (SaaS) offerings. This security control involves configuring cloud infrastructure and services according to security best practices and established security baselines to reduce vulnerabilities and enhance resilience. Secure Configuration Management is at the core of Hardened Cloud. It focuses on hardening cloud infrastructure and services by applying secure configuration settings to protect against unauthorized access and other security risks. The main objective of Hardened Cloud is to establish a robust security posture by minimizing potential avenues of exploitation and ensuring that cloud environments are configured securely from the outset. In summary, Hardened Cloud is a security control that helps organizations protect their cloud-based assets and data from cyber threats and vulnerabilities by applying secure configuration settings. By adhering to hardening guidelines and best practices, organizations can enhance the resilience and security posture of their cloud environments.
source_citations: FAIR-CAM Controls Library (docs/reference/fair-cam-controls-library.csv): name/description/type/cost + FAIR-CAM sub-function paths.
NIST CSF 1.1 / CIS v8 framework tags manually curated (validate-not-derive); FAIR-CAM grounding verified via the P2a NIST+CIS crosswalk.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate; CIS/MITRE grounds channel, not magnitude): Hardened Cloud configuration removes public network exposure of cloud assets — blocks public object-storage (S3 Block Public Access), restricts security-group ingress (removes 0.0.0.0/0 on RDP/SSH), and tightens IAM resource policies — removing unauthenticated contact between external threat agents and cloud data stores / compute. Asset = cloud data stores / compute; susceptibility = network reachability / public exposure; threat = external actors enumerating exposed cloud resources. Channel per rubric §7.3 CSPM worked example (avoidance dominant for cloud exposure) + §4-I1. Channel grounding: MITRE ATT&CK T1530 (Data from Cloud Storage) + M1037 (Filter Network Traffic); CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark — S3 Block Public Access. Crosswalk grounding: NIST CSF PR.AC-5 (network integrity / segregation — restrict SG ingress) + CIS Safeguard 3.3 (Configure Data Access Control Lists — block public storage), both genuine Hardened-Cloud homes that already crosswalk to lec_prev_avoidance (no extension needed). Magnitude 0.6 conservative expert estimate.

entries.name: Hardened Operating System and Services (HAOS)
entries.description: Hardened Operating System and Services refers to the application of secure configuration settings to operating systems and their associated services. This security control involves configuring the OS and services according to security best practices and established security baselines to minimize vulnerabilities and reduce the attack surface. Secure Configuration Management is at the core of HAOS. It focuses on hardening the OS and services by applying secure configuration settings to enhance resilience and protect against unauthorized access and other security risks. The main objective of HAOS is to strengthen the security posture of the operating system and services through secure configuration settings. This includes removing unnecessary services, disabling unused features, and ensuring that the OS and services are configured securely from the outset. In summary, Hardened Operating System and Services is a security control focused on applying secure configuration settings to operating systems and services. By adhering to hardening guidelines and best practices, organizations can enhance the resilience and security posture of their IT infrastructure.
source_citations: FAIR-CAM Controls Library (docs/reference/fair-cam-controls-library.csv): name/description/type/cost + FAIR-CAM sub-function paths.
NIST CSF 1.1 / CIS v8 framework tags manually curated (validate-not-derive); FAIR-CAM grounding verified via the P2a NIST+CIS crosswalk.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate; CIS/MITRE grounds channel, not magnitude): HAOS removes unnecessary services, disables unused features, and closes default listening ports on the operating system, reducing the attack surface — i.e. removing threat-contact vectors between external threat agents and the host's services (fewer reachable services = fewer contact points). Asset = the operating system / host and its services; susceptibility = number of reachable/enabled services (attack surface); threat = external actors enumerating and reaching exposed OS services. Channel per rubric §4-I1 (attack-surface removal removes contact → LEC Avoidance / reduces TEF, distinct from Example-3 Resistance which reduces exploitability after contact). Channel grounding: MITRE ATT&CK T1046 (Network Service Discovery) + M1042 (Disable or Remove Feature or Program); CIS Controls v8 Safeguard 4.8 (Uninstall or Disable Unnecessary Services). Crosswalk grounding: CIS Safeguard 4.8 via the REVIEWED RiskFlow crosswalk-seed extension 4.8 -> lec_prev_avoidance (#437 rollout T2; collateral = HAOS + HASS only). Magnitude 0.6 conservative expert estimate — MITRE/CIS give qualitative surface-reduction guidance, not a measured [0,1] contact-reduction fraction.

entries.name: Hardened SaaS Application (HASS)
entries.description: Hardened SaaS Application refers to the implementation of secure configuration settings to enhance the security posture of SaaS applications, ensuring the protection of sensitive data, applications, and resources hosted in the cloud. This security control involves configuring SaaS applications according to security best practices and hardening guidelines to mitigate the risk of data breaches, unauthorized access, and other security threats associated with SaaS deployments. Secure Configuration Management is at the core of Hardened SaaS Applications. It focuses on hardening cloud-based software solutions by applying secure configuration settings to protect against cyber threats and vulnerabilities. This includes ensuring that SaaS applications adhere to industry best practices, security standards, and compliance requirements to maintain the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data and services. The main objective of Hardened SaaS Applications is to establish a secure environment for SaaS deployments, mitigating the risk of security incidents and data breaches that could compromise sensitive information and disrupt business operations. In summary, Hardened SaaS Applications are a security control that helps organizations protect their cloud-based software solutions from cyber threats and vulnerabilities by applying secure configuration settings. By adhering to hardening guidelines and best practices, organizations can enhance the security posture of their SaaS applications, reduce the risk of data breaches and security incidents, and maintain the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of their data and services.

capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate; CIS/MITRE grounds channel, not magnitude): Hardened SaaS restricts public / external sharing and access on the SaaS application — disables public link-sharing, removes anonymous / external access, and enforces domain-restricted sharing — removing external unauthenticated contact with SaaS data. Asset = SaaS data stores; susceptibility = public share links / over-broad external access; threat = external actors accessing shared SaaS data. Channel per rubric §7 CSPM worked example (mirrors SSPM/SCA avoidance grounding) + §4-I1. Magnitude 0.6 conservative expert estimate.

entries.name: Host Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (HIDS)
entries.description: Host Intrusion Detection and Prevention system is a security control designed to monitor and protect individual endpoints, such as servers, workstations, and other devices, from unauthorized access, malicious activities, and security breaches. This control involves deploying specialized software agents on host systems to monitor system activity, detect suspicious behavior, and prevent malicious activities in real-time. At its core, Host Intrusion Detection and Prevention aims to identify and respond to security incidents on individual endpoints by analyzing system logs, network traffic, and file integrity to detect compromise and unauthorized access indicators. HIDP solutions employ various detection techniques, including signature-based detection, anomaly detection, and behavior analysis, to identify potential security threats and intrusions. One of the main objectives of Host Intrusion Detection and Prevention is to provide visibility and control over endpoint security, enabling organizations to detect and respond to security incidents quickly and effectively. Implementing Host Intrusion Detection and Prevention requires deploying specialized software agents on individual endpoints, configuring detection rules and policies, and integrating HIDP solutions with other security controls and systems, such as SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) platforms and security orchestration tools. In summary, Host Intrusion Detection and Prevention is a security control that helps organizations protect individual endpoints from unauthorized access, malicious activities, and security breaches. By continuously monitoring system activity, detecting suspicious behavior, and responding to security incidents in real time, HIDP solutions enhance the security posture of organizations and reduce the risk of data breaches and cyber attacks targeting host systems.

entries.name: Incident Response (IR)
entries.description: Incident Response is a systematic approach to managing and mitigating security incidents, such as cyber-attacks, data breaches, and unauthorized access, to minimize their impact on an organization's operations, data, and reputation. This security control involves establishing predefined procedures, protocols, and processes to assess, contain, eradicate, and recover from security incidents promptly and effectively. At its core, Incident Response aims to enable organizations to respond promptly and decisively to security incidents by providing a structured framework for identifying, analyzing, and responding to security threats and breaches. It involves establishing roles and responsibilities, defining escalation procedures, and implementing communication channels to facilitate coordination and collaboration among incident response team members. One of the main objectives of Incident Response is to reduce the impact of security incidents on an organization's operations, data, and reputation by minimizing downtime, data loss, and financial losses associated with security breaches. Additionally, effective Incident Response capabilities reduce attacker dwell time, which consequently limits the attacker's opportunity to inflict more damage. In summary, Incident Response is a security control that helps organizations respond to and recover from security incidents. By establishing predefined procedures, protocols, and processes for incident analysis, containment, eradication, and recovery, organizations can minimize the impact of security breaches and maintain the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of their data and systems.

entries.name: Mobile Device Management (MDM)
entries.description: Mobile Device Management is a security control focused on managing and securing mobile devices, such as smartphones, tablets, and laptops, used by employees within an organization. This control involves deploying software and implementing policies and procedures to monitor, manage, and protect mobile devices and the data they access, store, and transmit. At its core, Mobile Device Management aims to address the security challenges posed by the proliferation of mobile devices in the workplace, including the risk of data loss, unauthorized access, and malware infections. By centralizing device management and enforcing security policies, MDM solutions enable organizations to maintain visibility and control over mobile devices and mitigate the risks associated with their use. One of the main objectives of Mobile Device Management is to ensure the security and integrity of corporate data on mobile devices by implementing measures to enforce security policies, control device access, and protect against threats. It involves remotely configuring devices, enforcing encryption, enforcing passcode policies, and implementing containerization to separate corporate and personal data. In summary, Mobile Device Management is a security control that helps organizations secure and manage mobile devices in the workplace. By implementing policies, procedures, and technologies to monitor, manage, and protect mobile devices, organizations can reduce the risk of data breaches, unauthorized access, and other security threats associated with using mobile technology.

entries.name: Multi-factor Authentication (MFA)
entries.description: Multi-factor authentication is a security control designed to enhance the security of user authentication by requiring users to provide multiple forms of verification before gaining access to a system, application, or service. At its core, Multi-factor authentication aims to address the limitations of traditional password-based authentication, which can be vulnerable to various security threats, including password guessing, phishing attacks, credential stuffing, and credential theft. By requiring additional verification beyond just a password, MFA significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access and strengthens the overall security posture of an organization's authentication mechanism. Multi-factor authentication encompasses several common authentication factors, including: 1. Something the user knows: This typically refers to a password, passphrase, or personal identification number (PIN) that the user must enter during the authentication process. 2. Something the user has: This may include a physical device, such as a smartphone, smart card, or security token, that the user possesses and uses to generate or receive authentication codes or tokens. 3. Something the user is: This involves biometric authentication methods, such as fingerprint scanning, facial recognition, or iris scanning, that use unique physical characteristics to verify the user's identity. By requiring users to provide multiple forms of authentication, Multi-factor authentication significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access and strengthens the overall security of an organization's authentication process. Even if one factor is compromised or stolen, attackers would still need to bypass additional authentication measures to gain access, making it significantly more challenging for them to succeed. Implementing Multi-factor authentication requires organizations to deploy appropriate authentication mechanisms, such as one-time passwords (OTP), biometric scanners, and hardware tokens, and integrate them into their existing authentication systems and workflows. In summary, Multi-factor authentication is a security control that helps organizations strengthen the security of their authentication processes by requiring users to provide multiple forms of verification before gaining access to systems, applications, or services. By adding a layer of protection beyond just passwords, MFA significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access and helps protect sensitive information and resources from security threats.

entries.name: Network Access Control (NAC)
entries.description: Network Access Control is a security control designed to regulate and manage access to a network based on the identity, security posture, and compliance status of devices and users attempting to connect to it. This control involves implementing policies, technologies, and procedures to ensure that only authorized and compliant devices and users access the network and unauthorized or non-compliant devices are either blocked or placed in a restricted environment. At its core, Network Access Control aims to enforce security policies and mitigate security risks by controlling and monitoring access to network resources, data, and services. By dynamically assessing and implementing access policies based on the context of each connection attempt, NAC helps organizations prevent unauthorized access, protect against malware infections, and ensure compliance with security policies and regulatory requirements. One of the main objectives of Network Access Control is to provide comprehensive visibility and control over network access, allowing organizations to identify and respond to security threats and vulnerabilities in real-time. Implementing Network Access Control requires organizations to deploy NAC solutions, configure access policies, and integrate them into their existing network infrastructure and security architecture. In summary, Network Access Control is a security control that helps organizations regulate and manage access to their network resources and services. By dynamically assessing and enforcing access policies based on user identity, device security posture, and compliance status, NAC helps organizations prevent unauthorized access, protect against security threats, and ensure compliance with security policies and regulations.
