capability_citations: Mechanism (expert-estimate; NIST/MITRE grounds channel, not magnitude): the 'AND Encryption' half of the control — WPA2/WPA3 cryptographic protection of wireless data-in-transit — reduces the probability that an on-path (adversary-in-the-middle) threat agent within RF range can read or manipulate wireless traffic after contact. Asset = wireless data-in-transit; susceptibility = exploitability of the radio link (cleartext / weak-cipher interception); threat = eavesdropping / on-path manipulation. Channel per rubric §4-I1 Example-3 (reduces exploit probability after contact → LEC Resistance / reduces Vulnerability). Channel grounding: MITRE ATT&CK T1040 (Network Sniffing) + T1557 (Adversary-in-the-Middle) with mitigation M1041 (Encrypt Sensitive Information); NIST SP 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity). Crosswalk grounding: NIST CSF PR.DS-2 (Data-in-transit is protected), a genuine encryption home that already crosswalks to lec_prev_resistance (no extension needed). Magnitude 0.7 expert estimate — WPA3 is strong crypto but downgrade / misconfig and legacy WPA2 leave residual exploitability; ≤0.8 ceiling.

entries.name: System Capacity Planning (CAP)
entries.description: System Capacity Planning is a security control focused on ensuring that an organization's IT infrastructure has sufficient resources to support its operational needs while maintaining adequate levels of security. It involves forecasting and allocating resources such as processing power, memory, storage, and network bandwidth to meet current and future demand. System Capacity Planning helps prevent performance bottlenecks, downtime, and security vulnerabilities arising from resource constraints by proactively assessing the organization's current and projected resource requirements. It ensures systems can handle peak loads and unexpected surges in demand without compromising security or service availability. Capacity Planning considers factors such as application usage patterns, data growth rates, business growth projections, and security requirements to determine the optimal allocation of resources. It includes identifying potential points of failure and implementing redundancy measures to enhance resilience and mitigate the risk of service disruptions. From a security perspective, Capacity Planning helps organizations anticipate and address potential security risks associated with resource constraints, such as overloaded servers or network congestion, which attackers could exploit to launch cyberattacks or compromise system integrity. In summary, System Capacity Planning is a security control that aligns IT resource allocation with business objectives while safeguarding against security threats and ensuring the reliability and performance of critical systems and services. By proactively managing system capacity, organizations can enhance their resilience to security risks and maintain a robust security posture in today's dynamic threat landscape.

entries.name: Managed Detection and Response (MDR / MSSP)
entries.description: Managed Detection and Response (MDR) is an outsourced security operations service that combines 24/7 monitoring of endpoint, network, and cloud telemetry with expert-led triage, proactive threat hunting, and hands-on incident containment. An MDR provider (or managed security service provider, MSSP) operates the detection stack on the customer's behalf: continuously collecting and correlating security events, recognizing attack patterns against curated detection content and threat intelligence, investigating alerts, and executing or guiding response actions such as host isolation, credential revocation, and malicious-process termination. For organizations without a staffed security operations center — the common case for small and mid-sized teams — MDR supplies the around-the-clock detection-and-response capability that in-house tooling alone cannot deliver, shortening the interval between compromise, discovery, and containment. Proactive threat hunting is included in this entry: hypothesis-driven searches across collected telemetry for attacker behavior that evaded automated detection are a standard component of MDR service tiers rather than a separate product. When modeling a portfolio, note that MDR overlaps the detection channel of the tools it operates (EDR, SIEM): select MDR alongside those entries only when they represent genuinely distinct coverage, to avoid double-counting the same detection capability.
source_citations: Gap evidence (motivation only, associational): IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2025, Fig 39 pp.42-43 — MSSP factor -$128,087; proactive threat hunting factor -$193,242 (folded into this entry per #459 D3). See docs/reference/calibration-sources/ibm_codb_2025.md for caveats.

Function decomposition per docs/reference/control-function-decomposition-rubric.md (#437): 24/7 telemetry collection -> LEC Det Visibility; continuous monitoring/log review -> LEC Det Monitoring; alert correlation, detection content, hunting -> LEC Det Recognition; managed containment (isolation, termination) -> LEC Resp EventTermination. LEC Resp Resilience deliberately NOT claimed (plan-gate Meth-I1): recovery in an MDR engagement is guided/advisory - the customer executes - and the in-house precedents (incident-response, SOAR) claim event_termination only; claiming resilience would add unbacked v(S) magnitude. Same reasoning excludes lec_prev_resistance (endpoint hardening belongs to the underlying EDR tool entry - avoids double-counting).
NIST CSF 1.1 / CIS v8 framework tags manually curated (validate-not-derive); FAIR-CAM grounding verified via the P2a NIST+CIS crosswalk.

reference_annual_cost: 'Technical - detection' bucket (USD 100,000/yr) per docs/reference/fair-cam-controls-library-cost-methodology.md - vendor fee + retained internal liaison ops; same class as EDR/SIEM/SOAR.

entries.name: External Attack Surface Management (EASM)
entries.description: External Attack Surface Management (EASM) continuously discovers, inventories, and monitors an organization's internet-facing assets from an attacker's outside-in perspective: domains and subdomains, IP ranges, cloud services, exposed applications, certificates, and third-party-hosted assets, including the unknown and unmanaged systems that internal inventories miss. EASM platforms probe this external surface for exposures - open services, expired or misconfigured certificates, vulnerable software versions, shadow-IT deployments - and prioritize the findings so that remediation can be routed to owners. As an identification-side control, EASM tells an organization where its hardening and patching controls are silently absent or drifting on the assets attackers can actually reach; the correction itself (decommissioning, patching, hardening) is carried out by the organization's other controls and processes. It complements internal vulnerability assessment, which scans known assets from the inside, by finding the assets nobody knew to scan.

Function decomposition per docs/reference/control-function-decomposition-rubric.md (#437): external asset/exposure discovery = monitoring where OTHER controls are absent or drifted on reachable assets -> VMC Identification (ControlMonitoring), meta channel; identification-only by product boundary (correction is out-of-product), so non-scoring standalone by topology - same honest profile as Vulnerability Assessment (vmc_id_control_monitoring, single claim).
